title: Family-level factors contributing to diet behaviors among Hispanics: opportunities for nutrition intervention
keywords: families preparing the new generation plus
rct (fpng plus parenting intervention; n=235 dyads)
observational association study (feeding practices vs cardiometabolic risk; n=61 dyads)

summary.hook: This poster argues that family context is a key leverage point for improving diet behaviors and cardiometabolic health among Hispanic/Latinx households.
summary.summary_30s: This poster argues that family context is a key leverage point for improving diet behaviors and cardiometabolic health among Hispanic/Latinx households. It summarizes three lines of work from the Community Nutrition and Cardiometabolic Health Laboratory: (1) determinants of diet quality, (2) the home food environment as a target for intervention, and (3) culturally relevant interventions. Findings highlighted include results from the Athletes for Life community/family trial (n=131 households) examining home food availability changes, and from a 10-week parenting intervention trial (n=235 dyads) plus observational associations between feeding practices and adolescent cardiometabolic risk factors (n=61 dyads). Overall, the poster concludes that focusing on family health and household context is a promising approach for promoting healthy eating among Hispanics.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Describe why family and household context matter for diet behaviors and cardiometabolic risk among Hispanics/Latinx families.

Approach: Population: * Families with elementary school-aged children (Athletes for Life; n=131 households). Primary outcome: Not stated on poster (varies by study; poster emphasizes home food availability and adolescent cardiometabolic risk factors).

Finding: Family matters because lifestyle behaviors are learned at home and shared; interventions can affect multiple family members; motivation includes being present for family; parents shape home food environment through roles and practices; family is central in Hispanic support structures.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: Focusing on family health and the broad household environmental context is a promising approach for promoting healthy eating among Hispanics. Implications (poster-grounded): Interventions that modify the home food environment may influence availability of fruits/vegetables and sugar-containing foods in Hispanic households. Parenting practices and mealtime context (e.g., media use, restrictive feeding) may be meaningful targets for reducing adolescent cardiometabolic risk factors in Latinx families. Cultural sensitivity is emphasized: family plays a central role in support structures among Hispanic communities, suggesting family-based approaches are appropriate. Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Parents shape health behaviors by modeling behavior, providing resources, shaping the home food environment, setting rules/feeding practices, and influencing family meals and food availability. * Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): Five references are listed, including two Contemporary Clinical Trials papers (Athletes for Life; parenting intervention rationale), one Nutrients RCT outcomes paper (2023), one Journal of Nutrition paper (submitted), and one AHA Scientific Sessions abstract/presentation (2020).
key_numbers: n=131 households; n=235 dyads; n=61 dyads.
Not stated on poster (parenting trial described as 10-week; multiple measurement time points T1/T2/T3 shown).
* Families with elementary school-aged children (Athletes for Life; n=131 households).
Use of media during meals associated with adolescent weight (B=7.8, r=0.31, p=0.02) and BMIz (B=0.37, r=0.27, p=0.05).
Parental restrictive feeding style associated with adolescent weight (B=5.5, r=0.22, p=0.03), BMI% (B=13.2, r=0.52, p=0.001), and DBP (B=2.5, r=0.27, p=0.04).
Parental energy index associated with adolescent weight (B=6.9, r=0.46, p=0.001) and BMI% (B=6.3, r=0.33, p=0.01).
takeaways: Family matters because lifestyle behaviors are learned at home and shared; interventions can affect multiple family members; motivation includes being present for family; parents shape home food environment through roles and practices; family is central in Hispanic support structures.

Feeding practice associations (n=61 dyads): use of media during meals is associated with adolescent weight and BMI z-score; parental restrictive feeding style is associated with adolescent weight, BMI%, and DBP; parental energy index is associated with adolescent weight and BMI%.
Family meal frequency/fast food frequency/importance of family meals and inclusion of several foods/beverages during family meals were not associated with adolescent risk factors (as stated).
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: For the n=61 dyad associations: what regression models were used, and what covariates were included (child age/sex, SES, etc.)?

what_to_ask.question: In the n=61 dyads, what does B=7.8 for media use during meals → weight represent (units of weight and unit increase in media use)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: Were the feeding-practice associations adjusted for household income (\~$20,000) and household size (5.3), given potential confounding?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: Based on your findings, what family-level intervention components are most promising (reduce media during meals, adjust restrictive feeding, change availability)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.

collaboration_ideas.idea: Test a more focused intervention targeting mealtime media use, given its association with weight/BMIz
collaboration_ideas.ideal_partner: Partner aligned with the poster's next-step question
collaboration_ideas.what_they_contribute: Access, implementation context, data, or methods needed for the next phase named on the poster.
collaboration_ideas.what_they_get: A concrete role in shaping and evaluating the poster's next step.

collaboration_ideas.idea: Collaborate with clinics to integrate feeding-practice screening into pediatric visits for Latinx adolescents
